description: v9-fixes-2 - second hardening wave for the human-multiplayer layer. US-189..195 (DONE, merged-pending) addressed the residual findings from the post-merge /code-review re-run. US-196..203 (ROUND-2) fix the 13 actionable bugs surfaced by the independent adversarial review of the US-189..195 diff (1 critical chat-release replay-gap, 4 high: launch admission leak + reservation double-count + takeover rollback + migration 0045 dedup, 3 medium: presence monotonicity + takeover presence-lock + OAuth state-key, 5 low: OAuth azp/iat-age/single-use-state + rate-limit peek + profile fraction-string). US-204..209 (ROUND-3) fix the 6 regressions the final re-review found in the ROUND-2 fixes (R1 rate-limit peek-then-commit dropped the atomic decision -> concurrent over-admission; R2 migration 0045 dedup collides on league_id-scoped unique constraints; R3 takeover apply-after-commit DB-ahead-of-memory gap; R4 OAuth iat max-age too strict + not configurable; R5 OAuth single-use claim rolled back on failed exchange; R6 oauth_consumed_flows prune in the hot path). The 13 original findings are all confirmed resolved. Once US-204..209 are green and re-reviewed, this branch merges to main. Recommended to run BEFORE the Wave 10 role-expansion (prd-v10-roles.json) so the human-MP foundation is hardened before it is extended.

userStories.title: Atomic event persistence for takeover + chat-release (event-log-wins on crash)
userStories.description: FINDING 1 (most significant). _take_over_expired_human_seats commits the GameSeat change (seat_kind=AI_TAKEOVER, takeover fields) in its own transaction, but the paired SeatTakenOver event is only appended to the in-memory event_log and persisted to game_events later by drive_game_loop's persist_pending_events (after the release-delay sleep). Likewise release_held_chat_for_phase commits the sidecar row + marks the hold RELEASED in its own txn while the content_ref chat event is persisted to game_events only later. A process crash in that window leaves the DB AHEAD of the hash chain: a seat is AI_TAKEOVER (or a sidecar row exists at sequence N) with no corresponding game_events row, so on rehydrate the event-log replay reconstructs a DIFFERENT state (takeover provenance lost; or the next chat release re-derives sequence N and collides on uq_human_chat_message_sequence, wedging chat). This soft-violates hard rule 4 (event log is the source of truth).
acceptanceCriteria: The takeover seat mutation (seat_kind/takeover fields) and its SeatTakenOver game_events row are persisted in a SINGLE transaction (so the seat row is never committed without its event row).

A released human chat message's sidecar row + hold status flip and its content_ref game_events row are persisted in a SINGLE transaction (sidecar sequence and event sequence cannot diverge across a crash).

A test proves the invariant: for any committed takeover/chat-release DB mutation there is a matching game_events row at the expected sequence; simulate an interrupted run (apply the seat/sidecar mutation, then rehydrate from game_events) and assert reconstructed state is consistent (no AI_TAKEOVER seat missing its SeatTakenOver; no orphaned sidecar row; chat release resumes without a uq collision).
All backend gates pass (ruff/format/mypy/pytest -m 'not integration').
userStories.notes: Cleanest fix: append+persist the event in the same session/txn as the DB mutation (or persist_pending_events before returning control across the await/sleep). Keep replay deterministic.

userStories.title: Atomic inference-$ cap + breaker; release admission slots on abort
userStories.description: FINDING 2. US-165 made the per-user/day COUNT caps race-safe via the slot table + unique constraint, but the per-user daily inference-$ cap and the global cost breaker are still plain SELECT-sum reads compared to a threshold (TOCTOU): concurrent admissions all read sub-threshold spend and all pass, overshooting the $ budget. Separately, _claim_admission_slot inserts a slot per create/join/launch that is NEVER released, so an admitted-but-aborted/abandoned action permanently consumes a day-cap slot (the cap counts ATTEMPTS not games -> self-DoS, and a probe of the create endpoint can burn a victim's day budget since it is keyed only on principal_id).
acceptanceCriteria: The daily inference-$ cap and the global breaker are enforced atomically (reservation/lock or a slot-style accounting) so concurrent admissions cannot overshoot the $ ceiling; a concurrency test asserts the cap holds under N simultaneous admits.

Admission slots are tied to the resulting Game/LobbyMember (or released/expired on a failed/abandoned action) so the day caps count actual games/joins, not attempts; a test asserts an aborted create/join does not permanently consume a slot.
The breaker stays throttle-not-kill (never boots an active game).
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Sized to the Moderate $500-$2k/mo platform-absorbed envelope; the inference-$ cap is the one most worth making atomic.

userStories.title: Validate action legality BEFORE consuming the rate-limit budget
userStories.description: FINDING 3. In submit_action the order is idempotency -> _enforce_action_rate_limits (consumes a per-principal AND per-game-phase token) -> legal_actions_for validation. So an action that is rejected as illegal/out-of-phase (e.g. voting a target eliminated between observe and submit - a normal live-game race) still burns the seat's rate budget; repeated legitimate-but-now-illegal submissions can 429-lock the seat out of its ONE legal move for the phase.
acceptanceCriteria: Legality (allowed_action_types + target legality + terminal/phase) is validated BEFORE a rate-limit slot is consumed (or the slot is refunded on a validation rejection); a malformed/illegal action returns 409 without consuming the budget.
A test asserts a seat that submitted several now-illegal actions can still submit its valid action in the same phase (not 429-locked).
Order the cheap existence/terminal (404/409) checks and the presence heartbeat sensibly (do not heartbeat for an unknown/terminal game).
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Also covers the FINDING that record_heartbeat ran before the 404/terminal checks.

userStories.title: Race-safe presence heartbeat (atomic upsert)
userStories.description: FINDING 4. db/repositories/human_seat_presence.py record_heartbeat / mark_disconnected do read-then-insert with no DB-level upsert; concurrent heartbeats for the same (game_id, public_player_id) both see no row, both INSERT, and the second violates uq_human_seat_presence -> IntegrityError surfacing as a 500 on the presence/observation/action path. The chat-sequence allocator was given ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for exactly this; presence was left racy.
acceptanceCriteria: record_heartbeat / mark_disconnected use an atomic upsert (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (game_id, public_player_id) DO UPDATE) so concurrent heartbeats never raise IntegrityError.
A concurrency test fires simultaneous heartbeats for one seat and asserts no error + a single coherent presence row.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Keep portable across SQLite + Postgres (sqlalchemy dialect-aware upsert or insert-with-on-conflict helper).

userStories.title: OAuth id_token exp/iat hardening + dedicated state-signing key
userStories.description: FINDING 5. api/oauth.py claims_options marks iss/aud/sub/nonce essential but omits exp/iat, so claims.validate() only checks exp when present - a signature-valid id_token with no exp claim is accepted as a permanent, non-expiring login assertion. Separately, the CSRF/session-binding state HMAC is keyed on client_secret, coupling state integrity to the OAuth client secret (widens blast radius of a secret leak).
acceptanceCriteria: id_token validation marks exp essential (and validates iat with a small leeway) so a token without a bounded lifetime is REJECTED fail-closed; a test asserts an exp-less / expired token is rejected.
The state/session-binding HMAC uses a dedicated server signing key (e.g. a settings secret) rather than client_secret.
Existing OAuth tests (stubbed signed token/JWKS) still pass; add a regression test for the exp-less case.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Real provider creds remain a deploy-time step; validate logic against a stubbed signed token.

userStories.title: State-cache: preserve full tamper-detection + complete cache adoption
userStories.description: FINDING 6 (+7 partial). US-168's resolve_current_human_state / EventLog.from_stored trust the cached prefix bodies (from_stored does not recompute event_hash from each body; only the cached head is cross-checked against the DB head row and the suffix is re-sealed), so a tampered pre-head game_events body is not detected on the read path the way the old replay_state_from_rows (full re-seal) was. Defense-in-depth (needs DB write access), but the human request paths lost an integrity check. Also: human_turing.py still uses the pre-cache full replay (incomplete adoption), and human_game_runtime.upsert defaults state_cache=None and unconditionally overwrites it (a future partial-update caller silently wipes the cache).
acceptanceCriteria: The cache read path does not weaken tamper detection vs the old replay: either re-verify the cached prefix's chain (recompute event_hash) on load, or detect a mismatch and fall back to full verified replay; a test tampers a pre-head body and asserts it is detected (not silently served).
human_turing.py uses the shared resolve_current_human_state cache path (consistent with action/chat/observation), not a separate full replay.
human_game_runtime.upsert preserves an existing state_cache when the caller does not supply one (no accidental wipe); a test covers a cache-omitting update.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Balance integrity vs the US-168 performance win; the goal is no regression in tamper detection and a single state-resolution path.

userStories.title: Cleanup: dedup reveal seat-auth, dup-league constraint, hot-path + compat tidy
userStories.description: FINDINGS 7-8 + minors. api/reveal.py defines a private resolve_participant_seat identical to the shared api/human_seat_auth.resolve_human_game_seat added in the same wave (the reveal route - an identity-disclosure surface - was missed by the US-170 consolidation, so its seat-auth can drift). get_or_create_humans_included now scopes by ruleset_id but the leagues table has no unique (kind, ruleset_id) constraint, so concurrent calls can create duplicate dormant leagues. Minor: build_seat_observation_snapshot reads the human_game_runtime row twice; the reveal non-terminal fallback replays the full event log to return 409; resolve_human_game_seat binds on occupant_principal_id with no seat_kind filter; _accuracy_string now renders '2/3' not '0.666...' (confirm the dashboard renders it as a string, not parseFloat).
acceptanceCriteria: The reveal route uses the shared human_seat_auth.resolve_human_game_seat (single seat-auth source); the shared helper optionally restricts to human-lane seat kinds so the binding cannot be widened by an upstream invariant break.

A unique constraint on leagues (kind, ruleset_id) [scoped to HUMANS_INCLUDED] prevents duplicate dormant leagues; migration linear off the current head; get_or_create handles the conflict idempotently.
Observation reads the runtime row once (reuse the cache-resolution result for the deadline); the reveal non-terminal path avoids a full-log replay just to return 409.
Confirm/adjust the frontend reveal so accuracy is rendered as the server string (no parseFloat that would NaN on '2/3'); add a test.
All backend + frontend gates pass (pnpm lint/check/test:e2e if the dashboard is touched).
userStories.notes: Pure cleanup/robustness; no behavior change beyond removing drift and a duplicate-league race.

userStories.title: Close the chat-release replay-gap crash window (atomic sequence ordering)
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDING #1 (CRITICAL, CONFIRMED). In a human DAY discussion tick, run_human_tick first appends an ActionTimedOut/OutputInvalid failure event to the in-memory event_log at sequence N (persisted nowhere yet; tick.py:111,146-153). release_chat then runs in the SAME tick (human_lane.py:558-559), opens its own transaction (human_lane.py:725), appends a content_ref chat event at sequence N+1 and COMMITS its game_events row + sidecar + hold flip (human_chat_release.py:108-151). drive_game_loop only mirrors the lower failure event later via persist_pending_events(log_before) AFTER the tick returns (game_runner.py:867). A worker crash/cancel after the chat transaction commits but before persist_pending_events writes sequence N leaves game_events holding {... N-1, N+1} with N missing. On restart, rehydrate_active_human_games -> replay_event_log re-seals contiguously and raises ReplayHashMismatchError at the gap (replay.py:55-66; event_log.py:89-91), so the in-progress human game can never be resumed. The US-189 persisted-set skip only helps if persist_pending_events actually runs; it does NOT close the crash window between the N+1 co-commit and the persist of N.
acceptanceCriteria: INVARIANT: no transaction commits a game_events row at sequence N+1 while a lower-sequence event (N) produced earlier in the same tick exists only in the in-memory event_log. Concretely, the not-yet-persisted lower events (event_log from log_before up to the chat event's sequence) are persisted in the SAME transaction as the content_ref chat co-commit (option a: have release_chat first persist the pending lower events within its session.begin, then append its own row) OR persist_pending_events runs BEFORE release_chat and release_chat joins/extends that same transaction (option b).

Regression test: append an ActionTimedOut (or OutputInvalid) failure event at sequence N to the in-memory event_log, run release_chat to co-commit the content_ref chat event at N+1, simulate a crash BEFORE persist_pending_events(log_before) runs (i.e. do not call it), then run rehydrate_active_human_games / replay_event_log and assert it SUCCEEDS with a contiguous chain (no gap, no ReplayHashMismatchError).
The hash chain stays deterministic and replay-equals-live holds for the normal (non-crash) path; existing human-lane and chat-release tests still pass.
All backend gates pass (ruff/format/mypy/pytest -m 'not integration').
userStories.notes: Evidence: human_chat_release.py:108-151/119-124, human_lane.py:558-559/725, game_runner.py:867/250-258, tick.py:111,146-153, replay.py:55-66, event_log.py:89-91. The existing test (test_human_chat_release.py:154-235) only exercises the chat event as sequence 0 from an already-persisted log; it never covers the in-memory-failure-event-at-N-below-a-committed-N+1 gap. Keep replay deterministic.

userStories.title: Takeover in-memory/DB rollback consistency + double-append guard
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDING #4 (HIGH, PLAUSIBLE). take_over_seat (disconnect_takeover.py:75-92) performs two irreversible in-memory mutations - mux.swap_seat (multiplex.py:69-71, in-place dict write) and event_log.append (event_log.py:87-99, append-only, no rollback hook) - BEFORE _persist_stored_event_row (human_lane.py:451) does a plain INSERT (events.py:35-47, no ON CONFLICT), all inside the takeover session.begin() (human_lane.py:426). The mux/event_log/state are long-lived objects reused across ticks. If that transaction's flush/commit fails for ANY reason (connection drop, deadlock, serialization failure, uq_game_event_sequence collision), the DB rolls back seat.seat_kind to HUMAN and drops the event row, but the in-memory mux keeps the AI adapter and event_log keeps the SeatTakenOver. Two corruptions follow: (A) a later persist_pending_events re-inserts the orphaned in-memory event (its sequence is absent from persisted_sequences_from), and (B) the recheck _expired_human_seat_for_update only validates the DB seat (still HUMAN/expired) so the next tick calls take_over_seat AGAIN, appending a SECOND SeatTakenOver - corrupting provenance and the hash chain.
acceptanceCriteria: The in-memory mux.swap_seat + event_log.append for a takeover occur ONLY AFTER the co-committed DB write (seat mutation + SeatTakenOver row) commits successfully; OR they are reverted on transaction failure (swap the seat back to TakeoverResult.replaced_adapter and pop the just-appended event). The in-memory event_log/mux must never advance past what is durably committed in game_events.
The takeover recheck guards against re-taking a seat that already has an in-memory SeatTakenOver for that seat (no duplicate SeatTakenOver appended on a retry).

Regression test: force the takeover transaction's flush/commit to raise (patched session) and assert (a) the in-memory mux still routes the seat to the human adapter and event_log has NO orphaned SeatTakenOver, and (b) a subsequent tick does not append a second SeatTakenOver nor re-persist a rolled-back sequence.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: disconnect_takeover.py:75-92, human_lane.py:426-451/256-274, multiplex.py:69-71, event_log.py:87-99, events.py:35-47/106, game_runner.py:260-270. Existing tests (test_human_lane_takeover_wiring.py:440/497) only cover the commit-SUCCESS idempotency path and the crash-AFTER-commit path; the commit-FAILURE-mid-takeover window is unguarded. Cleanest: split take_over_seat into pure-envelope-build vs in-memory-commit; build+persist+commit, then apply to mux/event_log.

userStories.title: Admission slot lifecycle: bind/release launch slots + release reservations on completion
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDINGS #2 + #3 (HIGH, CONFIRMED). (a) The launch handler calls _enforce_admission(action=ACTION_LAUNCH) (lobbies.py:429) but DISCARDS the returned HumanAdmitDecision and never calls bind_admission_slots - unlike create_lobby (lobbies.py:194-201) and join_lobby (lobbies.py:314). admit_human on an allowed decision physically inserts one GAME-bucket HumanCostAdmission count slot + two HumanInferenceReservation rows (GLOBAL + per-user) with lobby_id/lobby_member_id NULL, so release_admission_for_lobby/_member can never free them; they stay LIVE for the whole reservation_day. ACTION_LAUNCH shares the GAME bucket with ACTION_CREATE (_admission_bucket), so a created+launched game double-counts the per-day game cap; and because admission runs before launch_lobby's idempotency short-circuit (lobby_launch.py:101-103), each idempotent re-launch leaks another set (self-DoS). (b) Inference reservations are released ONLY on abandon/kick/member-departure, never on launch or completion, while _claim_inference_slot ALSO counts charged spend via _implicit_budget_used (ceil(spent/reserve)) - the SAME dollars are counted twice (held reservation + charged implicit), so a user is denied daily_inference_cap_reached at roughly HALF the real $ cap.
acceptanceCriteria: The launch handler captures its HumanAdmitDecision and binds the slots to the launched lobby/host-member ONLY when launch_lobby materializes a NEW game (result.created); an idempotent re-launch (created=False) or a failed launch does NOT leak slots (move admission after the idempotency short-circuit, OR release/rollback the freshly claimed slots). A test asserts two POST /lobbies/{id}/launch leave exactly ONE live HumanCostAdmission game slot for the host.
A created+launched single game consumes exactly ONE game-bucket count slot against padrino_human_max_games_per_user_per_day (no create+launch double-count). A test covers this with games_per_day=1.

A game's inference reservations are released once its spend transitions to charged accounting (on launch and/or game completion) so held reservations and _implicit_budget_used(charged LlmCall spend) never count the same dollars twice. A test completes N games with real charged spend below the $ cap and asserts admission still succeeds (no premature daily_inference_cap_reached) until charged spend actually reaches the cap.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: lobbies.py:154/194-201/302/314/429/465/562/569, human_cost_governance.py:275-278/358/384-392/417/440/541-672/581-600/600-618, lobby_launch.py:101-103. bind_admission_slots, release_admission_for_lobby/_member. Prefer: short-circuit the already-LAUNCHED case BEFORE _enforce_admission (mirroring join_lobby's existing-guard), bind on result.created, and add a launch/completion reservation-release path analogous to release_admission_for_lobby.

userStories.title: Migration 0045: dedup duplicate dormant leagues before the unique index
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDING #5 (HIGH, PLAUSIBLE). Migration 0045 (0045_unique_humans_included_league.py:29-37) unconditionally runs op.create_index(..., unique=True) on leagues(ruleset_id) WHERE kind='HUMANS_INCLUDED' with NO preceding dedup. The pre-migration get_or_create_humans_included (base d831d1f) was a bare read-then-create with no DB constraint, so two concurrent callers could already have materialized duplicate HUMANS_INCLUDED leagues for the same ruleset_id in a deployed DB. On such a DB, CREATE UNIQUE INDEX raises a duplicate-key IntegrityError and ABORTS the upgrade. The new code's savepoint-based recovery (leagues.py:65-77) prevents FUTURE duplicates but relies on the index existing; it does not clean up pre-existing ones.
acceptanceCriteria: Migration 0045's upgrade() dedups HUMANS_INCLUDED leagues per ruleset_id BEFORE creating the unique index: keep the earliest (created_at, id), repoint any dependent rows (e.g. games.league_id and any other FK to leagues.id) to the keeper, then delete the duplicates - so upgrade succeeds on a DB that already contains duplicates.
Because 0045 is part of THIS unmerged wave, fold the dedup into 0045 in place; do NOT add a new revision (keep the migration chain linear, head stays 0045).

A migration test seeds duplicate HUMANS_INCLUDED leagues at the pre-0045 (0044) state, runs upgrade to 0045, and asserts it succeeds and leaves exactly one league per ruleset_id with dependents repointed; test_migrations_all_dialects still passes on all dialects.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: 0045_unique_humans_included_league.py:29-37 (docstring 'Existing rows stay byte-identical' is true but irrelevant to duplicate safety), leagues.py get_or_create_humans_included:65-77. If FK repointing is broad, at minimum repoint games.league_id; fail loudly rather than emitting a raw duplicate-key error if an un-repointable reference remains.

userStories.title: Presence monotonic upsert + takeover revalidation presence lock
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDINGS #6 + #7 (MEDIUM, PLAUSIBLE). (a) The presence ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (human_seat_presence.py:127-158) writes update_values UNCONDITIONALLY (no WHERE / GREATEST guard) for both Postgres and SQLite, so it is last-committer-wins independent of timestamp. record_heartbeat is called from three endpoints (human_actions, human_observation, human_chat); an older out-of-order heartbeat (last_seen=9 committing after last_seen=10), or a delayed mark_disconnected racing a newer record_heartbeat, clobbers the fresher row. The consumer _presence_from_row (human_lane.py:198-213) measures freshness from last_seen_at when connected, so a regressed last_seen_at or a stale connected->False flip can make the worker wrongly take a LIVE human seat over with AI. (b) _expired_human_seat_for_update (human_lane.py:256-274) takes with_for_update() on the GameSeat row but reads HumanSeatPresence with a plain unlocked SELECT (line 265). Under READ COMMITTED, a reconnect heartbeat that commits AFTER that read but BEFORE the takeover transaction commits is invisible, so the worker takes over despite the reconnect - contradicting the docstring guarantee (human_lane.py:370-372).
acceptanceCriteria: The presence upsert is MONOTONIC: an older write cannot regress last_seen_at nor flip a newer connected row to disconnected (e.g. on-conflict WHERE excluded.updated_at >= existing.updated_at, and/or last_seen_at = GREATEST(excluded, existing)), for BOTH Postgres and SQLite; the no-op (older-write-loses) branch still returns the current row rather than asserting it wrote.

Takeover revalidation serializes against reconnects: read the presence row FOR UPDATE in _expired_human_seat_for_update (or have record_heartbeat lock the GameSeat row) so a heartbeat racing the worker is serialized; after acquiring the lock, re-evaluate seats_past_grace before the seat flip.

Regression tests: (a) record_heartbeat(seen_at=older) committed after record_heartbeat(seen_at=newer) leaves last_seen_at at the newer value, and a stale mark_disconnected does not overwrite a strictly-newer connected heartbeat; (b) a reconnect heartbeat committing in the revalidation read->commit window cancels the takeover and keeps the human's turn.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: human_seat_presence.py:21-32/49-174/127-158, human_lane.py:256-274/198-213/370-372, human_observation.py:125, base.py (engine default READ COMMITTED). Existing tests (test_human_seat_presence_upsert.py; test_human_lane_takeover_wiring.py:348-411) only assert no-IntegrityError / single-row / heartbeat-before-revalidate; they do not cover monotonicity or the read->commit window. Option (a) presence FOR UPDATE in the revalidation is preferred (keeps the hot heartbeat path lock-light).

userStories.title: OAuth hardening: state-key fail-closed + azp/single-audience + iat max-age
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDING #8 (MEDIUM, CONFIRMED) + lows #10, #12. (a) When PADRINO_OAUTH_STATE_SIGNING_KEY is unset (settings.py:187 defaults None), _resolve_state_signing_key (oauth.py:132-141) falls back to base64url(HMAC(client_secret, b'padrino.oauth.state-signing-key.v1')) - a deterministic derivation from client_secret and a hardcoded, open-source domain string. A leaked client_secret therefore lets an attacker recompute the exact state-signing key and mint validly-signed CSRF/session-binding state tokens, defeating US-193's goal. The unit test only asserts byte-inequality (state_signing_key != client_secret), which passes even though the key is fully derivable. (b) claims_options sets no azp option (oauth.py:246-262); authlib's validate_aud accepts a multi-audience id_token as long as client_id is a member, so a token with aud=[client_id, other-rp] and azp=other-rp validates (OIDC requires azp==client_id when aud is multi-valued/azp present). (c) iat is essential but validate_iat only rejects future-dating (claims.py:197-211); there is no maximum token-age ceiling, so acceptance lifetime is bounded only by provider-controlled exp.
acceptanceCriteria: The OAuth state-signing key is FAIL-CLOSED: when OAuth is enabled, an unset/blank PADRINO_OAUTH_STATE_SIGNING_KEY is a config error (or yields no usable OAuth config so the routes 503) - NOT a silent derivation from client_secret. The state HMAC key is independent of client_secret. A test asserts the resolved key is NOT base64url(HMAC(client_secret, domain-string)) (non-derivability), replacing the byte-inequality-only assertion.

id_token validation enforces azp == client_id when aud is multi-valued OR azp is present (reject otherwise); a test signs an id_token with aud=[client_id, other-rp] and azp=other-rp and asserts the callback returns 400.

id_token validation enforces a MAXIMUM token age (reject if now - iat > a bounded ceiling, e.g. ~300s + leeway) using the injected clock seam; iat stays essential (the iat-essential strictness from US-193 is intended and retained). A test signs a stale-iat-but-unexpired (far-future exp) token and asserts rejection.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: oauth.py:123-141/246-263/341/365-369, settings.py:187, routes/human.py, test_oauth_signin.py:446-454/131/116-137/366-374. authlib JWTClaims.validate_aud/validate_iat. This is the api lane (pure-core firewall N/A); use the existing impure clock seam, never datetime.utcnow() in core. Do NOT relax iat-essential (finding #14 is intended).

userStories.title: OAuth single-use state store (replay-resistant callback)
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDING #11 (LOW, PLAUSIBLE). OAuth state and nonce are stateless signed tokens with no server-side single-use ledger: validate_authorization_state (oauth.py:169-193) only verifies the HMAC signature, version, nonce presence, and session_binding; it never records or checks a consumed-state set. The callback (routes/human.py:567-629) does compare_digest + validate_authorization_state + exchange_code with no DB/cache write marking the state/nonce/code as used. Within the 600s flow-cookie TTL, the same (state cookie, code) pair can be replayed; the only block on a second exchange minting a second session is the upstream provider invalidating the authorization code on first redemption (a provider-dependent defense). Add a server-side single-use block so replay fails closed independent of provider behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: A server-side single-use store keyed on the per-flow unique token (already generated in _encode_state, oauth.py:341) records consumed flows; the callback atomically inserts-or-rejects (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) BEFORE exchange_code, rejecting a replayed flow with a clear fail-closed error (e.g. oauth_state_replayed).
The store is short-TTL (about the 600s flow-cookie TTL) and prunable; rows past TTL are ignored or swept and do not block new flows.
Exactly ONE new linear Alembic revision (down_revision == the current head, 0045 after US-199) creates the table; portable across SQLite + Postgres; test_migrations_all_dialects passes.
Regression test: replaying the same (state cookie, code) twice yields a single successful session and a rejected second exchange.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: oauth.py:169-193/341, routes/human.py:567-629. Mirror the heartbeat ON CONFLICT upsert pattern for the atomic insert-or-reject. This is auth metadata (hash-chain rule 9 N/A). Keep severity-proportionate: a small short-TTL table, not a heavy session store.

userStories.title: Rate-limit peek-then-commit + profile accuracy fraction-string compat
userStories.description: ROUND-2 REVIEW FINDINGS #9 + #13 (LOW, PLAUSIBLE). (a) _enforce_action_rate_limits (human_actions.py:200-210) iterates buckets (principal_key, then game_phase_key) calling rate_limit.record_request, which INCREMENTS each bucket before returning a decision (InMemoryRateLimitStore:93; DatabaseRateLimitStore:137). So the per-principal bucket is incremented first; if the per-game-phase bucket is over limit the call raises 429 - but the principal-wide bucket was already burned for a request that produced no action. With per_game_phase=30 < per_principal=60, submits 31..60 in one phase burn principal slots while 429ing, over-consuming the seat's per-principal/minute budget across its OTHER games/phases. Same pattern in human_chat.py:253-254. (b) The dashboard profile pct() (profile/+page.svelte:21-25) does Number(value) and returns an em-dash on NaN; for a core fraction-string like '2/3' (core/turing/scoring.py _accuracy_string emits reduced ratios) Number('2/3')=NaN, so detection_accuracy would render an em-dash if ever wired to that format (the reveal page got the verbatim-render fix in this wave; profile did not).
acceptanceCriteria: Multi-bucket rate-limit enforcement is all-or-nothing: a rejection by a later bucket does NOT commit an earlier bucket's increment (add a non-incrementing peek/check to RateLimitStore and verify every bucket would be admitted before recording any, then record all) - for BOTH the action channel (human_actions.py) and the chat channel (human_chat.py). A test asserts a request rejected by the per-game-phase cap does not consume the per-principal budget.

The dashboard profile pct() tolerates the core 'num/den' fraction-string format (parse the ratio and format a percentage, or render the string verbatim) so a '2/3' detection_accuracy renders a value, not an em-dash; a frontend test covers it.
All backend gates pass; pnpm -C web/dashboard lint && check && test:e2e pass (the dashboard is touched).
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: human_actions.py:200-210, human_chat.py:253-254, rate_limit store (InMemory:93 / Database:137), profile/+page.svelte:21-25/99, core/turing/scoring.py:47-70 (_accuracy_string), reveal.spec.ts:249-253/340 (the format split + pinned '0.7500' decimal contract). Mixed backend+frontend story: run BOTH gate sets. Robust fix is the peek/commit split, not merely reversing bucket order.

userStories.title: Restore atomic rate-limit enforcement under peek-then-commit (honor record_request)
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R1 (HIGH, CONFIRMED) introduced by US-203. The peek-then-commit refactor gates admission on the read-only peek_request loop, then in a SECOND loop calls rate_limit.record_request(...) but DISCARDS the returned RateLimitDecision (human_actions.py:215-216, human_chat.py:267-268). peek_request is a separate non-locking SELECT (rate_limit_store.py:174-193). Under concurrency on the multi-worker DatabaseRateLimitStore (the designed-for case, app.py:82-87 selects it when padrino_api_workers>1), K requests at count==limit-1 all peek-pass, then all record (count -> limit+1...), and because the authoritative record_request rejection (new_count>limit) is thrown away, ALL are admitted. The original code (3c1d85a) was atomic increment-and-check (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE ... RETURNING count, then allowed=False if new_count>limit) and is explicitly tested for two concurrent workers admitting exactly limit (test_rate_limit_store.py:140-161). US-203 removed that atomic guarantee while fixing the lower-severity per-principal-budget-burn issue.
acceptanceCriteria: The commit phase HONORS the atomic decision: the record loop captures each record_request result and raises 429 if any bucket returns allowed=False (restoring the atomic increment-and-reject ceiling), for BOTH the action channel (human_actions.py) and the chat channel (human_chat.py). The peek pass that prevents burning the per-principal budget on a guaranteed-reject is retained.

A concurrency regression test against DatabaseRateLimitStore fires K simultaneous _enforce_*_rate_limits calls sharing one principal bucket at count==limit-1 and asserts at most limit are admitted (no over-admission).
The US-191/US-203 property still holds: a request rejected by the per-game-phase bucket does not consume the per-principal budget (the all-or-nothing peek still guards the common reject path).
All backend gates pass (ruff/format/mypy/pytest -m 'not integration').
userStories.notes: Evidence: rate_limit_store.py:150-238/174-193, human_actions.py:200-216, human_chat.py:249-268, app.py:82-87. The robust fix is an atomic multi-bucket check-and-increment (one transaction that increments all buckets and rolls back / returns the limiting decision if any exceeds) -- but at minimum the record loop must re-check the decision (do NOT discard it). Keep the peek loop for the budget-burn fix.

userStories.title: Migration 0045 dedup: resolve scope-unique collisions before repointing rating rows
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R2 (HIGH, CONFIRMED, reproduced) introduced by US-199. _dedup_humans_included_leagues blind-repoints every dependent FK row: UPDATE {table} SET league_id=:keeper WHERE league_id=:loser (0045_*.py:81-84) for all _DEPENDENT_LEAGUE_FKS, which includes ratings and human_rating. Those tables carry league_id-scoped UNIQUE constraints: uq_rating_scope=(league_id,agent_build_id,scope_type,scope_value) and uq_human_rating_scope=(league_id,human_player_id,scope_type,scope_value). On a dirty DB where a keeper and a loser HUMANS_INCLUDED league each hold a row for the same scope key, the repoint collides on the unique index and raises IntegrityError, ABORTING the very upgrade the dedup was added to rescue. Reproduced live: seed two HUMANS_INCLUDED leagues for mini7_v1 each with a human_rating row for (player1,GLOBAL,all), then alembic 0044->0045 fails with UNIQUE constraint failed: human_rating(...). (Note: rating_events/human_rating_event are keyed on game_id, NOT league_id, so they do not collide; gauntlets/lobbies have no league_id-scoped unique, so they are safe.)
acceptanceCriteria: Before repointing into a table whose UNIQUE constraint includes league_id (ratings, human_rating), the dedup first removes the loser rows whose remaining scope tuple already exists under the keeper (e.g. DELETE FROM ratings WHERE league_id=:loser AND (agent_build_id,scope_type,scope_value) IN (SELECT ... FROM ratings WHERE league_id=:keeper); analogously for human_rating on (human_player_id,scope_type,scope_value)), then repoints the survivors. Plain repoint remains for gauntlets/lobbies/rating_events/human_rating_event.
Folded into 0045 in place (no new revision; chain stays single-head at 0046).

A migration test seeds COLLIDING ratings and human_rating rows under both keeper and loser HUMANS_INCLUDED leagues and asserts the upgrade SUCCEEDS with the keeper's scope row retained (the existing test only seeds a collision-safe gauntlets row).
All backend gates pass (including test_migrations_all_dialects).
userStories.notes: Evidence: 0045_*.py:42-49/81-84, 0003_ratings.py uq_rating_scope, 0025_human_rating_siblings.py uq_human_rating_scope, models.py:390-396/511-517, tests/db/test_migration_0045_dedup.py:96-105. Per AGENTS.md rule 8 these tables are dormant in v1 so the collision is not reachable on a pure-v1 DB, but the migration must be defensively correct (fixtures / forward-port / v2 human-ELO activation make it live). A delete-loser-duplicate-scope-row is sufficient; merging stats into the keeper is optional.

userStories.title: Takeover apply-after-commit: guard against DB-ahead-of-memory on apply failure
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R3 (HIGH, PLAUSIBLE) introduced by US-197. US-197 correctly fixed the memory-ahead-of-DB direction by splitting build_takeover_event + _persist_stored_event_row (inside the session.begin() block, committing at human_lane.py:488) from apply_takeover (the mux.swap_seat + event_log.append) which now runs AFTER the commit at human_lane.py:492-498 with NO try/except. This newly creates the OPPOSITE gap: if apply_takeover raises after the commit (apply_takeover can raise a seal-mismatch ValueError or a mux.swap_seat KeyError, disconnect_takeover.py:104-132 / multiplex.py:64-68), the DB holds the SeatTakenOver row at sequence N but the in-memory event_log never appends N -> the next in-memory event is built at sequence N and collides on persist (IntegrityError), or rehydrate re-seals non-contiguously (ReplayHashMismatchError). NOT currently triggerable (no await between commit and apply; every human seat is registered in the long-lived mux; the recheck guards double-apply) -- but an unguarded latent crash-consistency fragility that a future change would activate.
acceptanceCriteria: A post-commit apply_takeover failure cannot leave the DB ahead of the in-memory log: wrap apply_takeover (human_lane.py:492-498) so that on any exception the in-memory state is deterministically re-synced to the already-committed row (re-append the committed event body after asserting it still seals, and force the mux swap) OR a dedicated fatal error halts the loop BEFORE any further event is built at the now-colliding sequence. Invariant: never a committed game_events row at sequence N with no matching in-memory event that a later tick will collide with.

A regression test forces a failure in the POST-commit apply_takeover step (monkeypatch apply_takeover / swap_seat to raise after the DB commit) and asserts the persisted chain and the in-memory log stay consistent and a subsequent tick does not collide / IntegrityError.
The US-197 memory-ahead protection and its existing test (test_takeover_commit_failure_leaves_in_memory_uncorrupted) still hold.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: human_lane.py:474-498/455, disconnect_takeover.py:104-132, multiplex.py:64-68, event_log.py:89. Symmetric closure of the build/apply-across-commit split US-197 introduced.

userStories.title: Configurable OAuth id_token max-age ceiling with a sane default
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R4 (HIGH, PLAUSIBLE) introduced by US-201. _validate_token_age (oauth.py:341-355) enforces a HARDCODED ceiling _MAX_TOKEN_AGE_SECONDS=300 (+60s leeway) with NO settings override: it rejects any signature-valid, unexpired id_token whose iat is older than 360s. A 6-minute ceiling is tight for a legitimate auth round-trip when provider/host clock skew exceeds the single 60s leeway, or for an IdP that mints iat earlier in the flow / from a quirky enterprise OIDC bridge -> a false-positive auth break (400 oauth_exchange_failed) for a valid login that worked before US-201, with no operator escape hatch. (Per OIDC iat is the mint time and major IdPs mint fresh, so the common case is fine; this hardens the false-positive risk, not removes the control.)
acceptanceCriteria: The max-token-age ceiling is operator-configurable: add padrino_oauth_max_token_age_seconds (int | None) to Settings, thread it into _validate_token_age via OAuthConfig; None disables the extra age check (exp still bounds the token). The hardcoded Final is replaced by the configured value.
The DEFAULT ceiling is widened to comfortably cover a legitimate slow authorization round-trip plus realistic clock skew (e.g. 600-900s), retaining iat-essential and the future-dating rejection.
Tests: a token older than the configured ceiling (far-future exp) is rejected; a token within the ceiling is accepted; setting the override (or None) changes the behavior accordingly.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: oauth.py:46-50/341-355/316/321, settings.py:170-187, tests/api/test_oauth_signin.py:699-730. Optionally make it auth_time-aware (do not reject on auth_time being old; bound strictly on iat). Use the impure clock seam (_NOW), never datetime.utcnow() in core. Document it as defense-in-depth supplementing exp, not an OIDC requirement.

userStories.title: Durable OAuth single-use flow claim across a failed code exchange
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R5 (MEDIUM, CONFIRMED) introduced by US-202. try_consume_flow (oauth_consumed_flows.py:64-66) only session.flush()es; the whole oauth_callback runs in one get_session transaction that rolls back on ANY raised exception (deps.py:24-30). So if exchange_code raises (human.py:612-615 -> 400) or find_or_create_account / create_session later raises, the consumed-flow INSERT is rolled back -> the flow is NOT recorded as consumed and a replay of that (state, code) is still re-attemptable. This contradicts the docstrings (models.py:845-847; human.py:587-590) that claim the row is recorded the moment the callback begins the code exchange and that a replayed flow fails closed independent of provider behavior. The atomic ON CONFLICT only blocks two concurrently in-flight replays; it does NOT give the durable single-use guarantee the docstrings assert for the failure paths.
acceptanceCriteria: Make the single-use guarantee match its contract: the consumed-flow claim is durable independent of the rest of the callback succeeding -- commit the claim in its own transaction / savepoint (or a separate session) right after try_consume_flow so a later rollback of exchange/account/session does not erase it; a replay of a (state, code) whose first attempt FAILED at/after exchange is rejected with oauth_state_replayed.
Exactly one consumed-flow row persists per flow even when the first callback fails after the claim.

A regression test drives a callback where exchange_code (or find_or_create_account / create_session) raises, then asserts a replay of the same (state, code) is rejected and exactly one consumed-flow row exists.
All backend gates pass.
userStories.notes: Evidence: oauth_consumed_flows.py:64-66, deps.py:22-30, human.py:587-590/601/605-629, models.py:845-847. If a durable autonomous commit is deemed too heavy, the acceptable alternative is to NARROW the docstrings to 'holds for successfully-completed flows; the atomic insert blocks concurrent in-flight replays' -- but durable consume is preferred since it is what the security claim promises.

userStories.title: Move oauth_consumed_flows prune out of the hot callback path + index
userStories.description: ROUND-3 REGRESSION R6 (LOW, CONFIRMED) introduced by US-202. Every OAuth callback unconditionally runs prune_expired -> DELETE FROM oauth_consumed_flows WHERE consumed_at < now-600 (human.py:598-600; oauth_consumed_flows.py:69-85), inside the request transaction and BEFORE the consume INSERT. There is NO index on consumed_at (migration 0046 / models.py:836-856 define only flow PK + a plain consumed_at column), so the predicate DELETE is a sequential scan on every sign-in; under concurrent load it serializes a delete-then-insert against the same table per request and the work is rolled back + repeated on any later failure. Write amplification + lock footprint per sign-in.
acceptanceCriteria: The per-callback hot path no longer issues an unconditional full-predicate DELETE every request: move the sweep to a periodic/background job OR an opportunistic probabilistic sweep (e.g. ~1/N callbacks) OR a separate short transaction outside the auth transaction; stale rows are documented as inert so deferring is functionally safe.

Add an index on oauth_consumed_flows.consumed_at so any remaining prune is an index range scan, not a sequential scan (edit migration 0046 in place since it is part of this unmerged wave; no new revision; chain stays single-head).
A test asserts pruning still bounds table growth (expired rows are eventually removed) without a DELETE on every callback.
All backend gates pass (including test_migrations_all_dialects).
userStories.notes: Evidence: human.py:598-600, oauth_consumed_flows.py:69-85, 0046_oauth_consumed_flows.py:30-33, models.py:836-856. The single-use INSERT/reject logic itself is correct -- leave it unchanged; this is purely the prune placement + index.
